The Reformation, the scientific revolution, and the rise of public opinion were the most important consequences of the invention of the printing press in early modern Europe.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Jazz improvisation looks, to a listener unfamiliar with the form, like pure invention from nothing. Players themselves tell a different story. The phrases that arrive in performance are drawn from a deep inventory of motifs, learned by ear, repeated until they reside below conscious thought. What is invented in the moment is the sequence — which phrase, in this harmony, against this rhythm — not the phrases themselves. The result is not lessened by this; rather, the apparent spontaneity is the visible portion of a hidden, lifelong accumulation. To call it free is to mistake the surface of mastery for its substance.
options: Jazz improvisation is a form of pure invention in performance, with phrases conjured from nothing and assembled in the moment.
Players draw their phrases from a deep, lifelong inventory of motifs learned by ear and stored below conscious thought.
Listeners admire jazz improvisation primarily because it appears spontaneous, even though the underlying performance is far more constrained than it sounds.
Improvisation only seems invented from nothing: players draw on a stored inventory and invent only the sequence — its freedom masks long-trained mastery.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Confident memories are felt as records, but they behave more like reconstructions. Each retrieval reassembles fragments and reweaves them in light of subsequent suggestion, mood, and inference; the act of remembering can itself alter the memory remembered. Witnesses who repeat their accounts in court do not merely report a stored film; they update a script. This is not a failure of attention but the normal operation of the system, which evolved for usefulness, not fidelity. Doubting one's most vivid recollections is not paranoia; it is calibration to how memory actually works.
options: Witnesses in court alter their testimony each time they repeat it, indicating that legal procedures should not rely on repeated witness statements.
Memory operates not as a record but as a reconstruction, which means that vivid recollections are less reliable than people assume and should always be corroborated.
Memory reconstructs rather than records; each retrieval updates the trace, so doubting one's most vivid recollections is not paranoia but calibration to the system's normal operation.
Because memory evolved for usefulness rather than fidelity, the human mind is poorly suited to recalling past events with the accuracy modern legal systems demand.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Critics of algorithmic feeds complain that they show users what users have already shown an interest in, creating echo chambers. The diagnosis is correct as far as it goes, but it misplaces the cause. The system optimises for engagement, not for ideological reinforcement; if outrage at the opposite tribe holds attention longer, the feed will deliver opposing views in deliberately inflammatory packaging. What looks like a bubble is sometimes a battlefield. Either way the result is the same — users grow more certain — but the prescription must address the metric, not the metaphor.
options: Whether by echo chambers or by inflammatory opposing-view delivery, algorithmic feeds increase certainty; engagement, not the echo-chamber metaphor, is the metric reform must target.
Algorithmic feeds create echo chambers by repeatedly showing users content they already agree with, which leads inevitably to increased polarisation across society.
Users encounter opposing views in their feeds far more often than echo-chamber critics realise, but those opposing views are packaged in deliberately inflammatory ways.
Engagement metrics on social platforms must be banned by regulators, since they reliably drive both echo-chamber and outrage dynamics that increase users' certainty.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

If a sugar pill genuinely relieves a patient's symptoms when described as medicine, is the doctor obligated to disclose its inertness, knowing the effect will collapse? The case is sharper than it first appears. The benefit is real; the means depends on a belief the patient would not have formed unaided. Yet a profession that traded routinely in such beliefs would erode the trust that lets honest information do its work in other cases. The placebo question therefore tests not only what we owe a particular patient but how we sustain a practice in which patients can take any of a doctor's claims at face value.
options: Doctors should never prescribe placebos, because doing so violates the patient's right to informed consent regardless of the therapeutic benefit produced.
The placebo effect proves that patient beliefs can substitute for active drugs in the relief of symptoms, suggesting medicine should make wider use of non-deceptive psychological interventions.
Although placebos relieve real symptoms, telling patients about them collapses the effect, so doctors face a difficult choice between honesty and benefit in each particular case.
Beyond a single patient, routine placebo use would corrode the trust on which honest medical communication depends — the question concerns not the individual alone but the practice's sustainability.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

A lichen was long described as a partnership of one fungus and one alga, the fungus providing structure and the alga providing sugars from photosynthesis. Recent genetic work has complicated that tidy picture: many lichens contain a second fungus, a basidiomycete, whose absence changes the morphology of the whole organism. What looked like a couple is in fact a small society, and an essential member of it was missed for over a century by methods that could see only what they were trained to find.
options: A lichen is a symbiosis of one fungus and one alga, in which the fungus provides structure and the alga provides sugars from photosynthesis.
Genetic work shows lichens often contain a second essential fungus the textbook missed — the two-partner picture was wrong, limited by methods that could see only what they sought.
Basidiomycete fungi in lichens contribute to the morphology of the organism, though their precise function remains under active investigation by researchers.
A lichen is best described not as a partnership but as a small society, since it contains numerous species each with a distinct functional role in the symbiosis.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Tipping is defended as a flexible reward for good service, but the empirical record undermines the defence. Studies find that tip size correlates more strongly with the customer's mood, the weather, and the server's appearance than with the speed or accuracy of the meal. The practice therefore does not do what its justification claims it does; it distributes income on grounds the system would, in other contexts, call discriminatory. That tipping persists is not evidence that it works; it is evidence that customs once embedded are remarkably hard to dislodge, even when the original case for them dissolves on examination.
options: Tipping doesn't reward service as defenders claim; tip sizes track mood, weather, and appearance — grounds we would otherwise call discriminatory — and the custom persists from inertia, not merit.
Tip sizes correlate more strongly with the customer's mood and the weather than with the speed or accuracy of the meal, indicating that customers tip in an arbitrary fashion.
Tipping persists because customs once embedded are very hard to dislodge, even when the original justification for those customs has been undermined by empirical research.
Restaurants should replace tipping with service-included pricing, since the existing practice distributes income on grounds the system would otherwise condemn as discriminatory.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Platforms that match riders and drivers or shoppers and stockers describe their workforces as independent contractors choosing when to work. The description hides where the discretion actually lies. Algorithms set prices, allocate jobs, rank performance, and decide who continues to receive work. The worker chooses, in any meaningful sense, only whether to log in. To frame this as self-employment is to borrow a category whose dignity rested on owning one's tools and pricing one's labour, neither of which now applies. The legal category lingers; the substance it once described is gone.
options: Platforms describe their workers as independent contractors who freely choose their hours, a characterisation that workers themselves generally accept.
Algorithms on gig platforms set prices, allocate jobs, rank performance, and decide who receives further work, leaving workers with only one real choice.
Gig platforms borrow self-employment's dignity for workers stripped of its substance — owning tools, pricing labour — since the platform's algorithms now control both.
Gig workers should be reclassified as employees and given access to the benefits and protections that traditional employment provides under existing law.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

To preserve a historic building one must constantly intervene: stones are replaced, beams resawn, paint matched as nearly as possible to the original. The question is whether what stands at the end is the building one set out to save. Strict purists insist on minimum intervention even at the cost of accelerating decay; pragmatists accept that a usable, mostly original building is more honest than a perfect ruin. The disagreement is less about technique than about what a building is — a fixed material object, or a continuing thread that requires renewal to remain itself.
options: Architectural preservation should always favour minimum intervention to maintain the maximum amount of original material in a heritage building.
Preservation disagreements rest less on technique than on which conception of a building one holds — a fixed object, or a thread that requires renewal.
Pragmatists in architectural preservation generally prefer a usable building over a perfect ruin, accepting more replacement of original material than purists would tolerate.
Stones, beams, and paint in historic buildings must be replaced periodically because no material survives indefinitely under the conditions of normal use.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

European feudalism is often said to have ended when monarchs centralised power; the timing of its decline, however, lines up better with the spread of cash rents. As soon as a lord accepted coin instead of labour from his tenants, he no longer needed the network of customary obligations that had bound them, and the tenants no longer needed the protection that justified those obligations. Both parties retained the language of duty long after the substance had become transactional. By the time monarchs claimed credit for breaking the system, the mortar holding it together had already crumbled.
options: Feudalism in Europe came to an end when monarchs successfully centralised power, dismantling the customary obligations between lords and tenants.
Lords gradually preferred coin payments to labour because coin was easier to spend, and this preference led inevitably to the disappearance of customary obligations.
The language of feudal duty persisted in Europe long after the substance had become transactional, indicating that ideological inertia is often slower than economic change.
Feudalism dissolved as cash rents loosened the mutual need obligations had served; centralising monarchs only finished off a system the cash economy had already hollowed out.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Public debate about driverless cars often turns on a contrived dilemma: should the vehicle, in a crash it cannot avoid, sacrifice its passenger to save several pedestrians? The thought experiment is more vivid than useful. Real crashes do not announce themselves in time for such calculations; the gains from automation will come from preventing the routine inattention, fatigue, and impairment that cause most deaths. By fixating on the rare scripted dilemma, the debate avoids the duller policy questions — testing protocols, insurance, infrastructure — on which the technology's actual safety record will depend.
options: Public debate on driverless cars dwells on contrived crash dilemmas while neglecting the duller policy questions — testing, insurance, infrastructure — that will actually determine the technology's safety.
Driverless cars in unavoidable crashes will need to choose between sacrificing their passenger and saving multiple pedestrians, raising hard ethical questions about how those choices should be made.
Most road deaths today are caused by inattention, fatigue, and impairment, and automation will sharply reduce these even before any ethical questions about crash priorities are resolved.
The trolley-problem dilemma applied to driverless cars is a useful thought experiment, even if real crashes rarely allow time for the deliberation it imagines.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Habits are widely sold as the way to install good behaviour without willpower: repeat an action long enough and it runs on its own. The advice has truth in it, but it understates a cost. A habit, once formed, is insensitive to whether the action is still serving its purpose. The morning run we adopted to escape stress continues after the stress is gone; the snack we trained ourselves to grab as a reward continues after the reward no longer needs marking. The very feature that makes habits useful — that they bypass deliberation — also makes them slow to update when the conditions that justified them have changed.
options: Habits are best installed by repeating a desired action consistently until it can be performed without willpower or deliberate effort.
The morning run and the snack ritual are examples of habits that have outlived their justification and therefore ought to be deliberately reconsidered.
Habits formed through repetition are inherently rigid and should be avoided wherever possible, since they prevent adaptive behaviour in changing circumstances.
The same feature that lets habits run without willpower — bypassing deliberation — also makes them slow to update when the conditions that justified them no longer hold.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Compatibilists hold that free will is consistent with determinism if 'free' is understood as acting on one's own desires without external coercion. Critics complain that this redefines the term until it loses its bite — surely free will meant something more than the absence of a gun to one's head. Compatibilists reply that the larger meaning was always incoherent. We could not have wanted otherwise without being a different person, and a will that operated independently of who one is would not be one's own. The argument does not vanquish the worry; it asks whether the worry was tracking a real distinction in the first place.
options: Compatibilists have decisively shown that free will and determinism are mutually consistent once 'free' is properly defined as acting on one's own desires without external coercion.
Compatibilism doesn't resolve the libertarian worry but reframes it — asking whether the original concept of freedom, independent of who one is, was ever coherent.
The dispute between compatibilists and their critics is essentially terminological, with the two sides using the word 'free' to mean different things and talking past each other.
Free will, properly considered, requires the absence of coercion and nothing more, since the demand for the ability to have wanted otherwise is incoherent on closer examination.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Migratory birds navigate over thousands of kilometres with a precision that long defied explanation. Compasses tuned to the sun and stars can drift; landmarks vanish over open water. Experiments in the last two decades suggest that the birds also sense the Earth's magnetic field, possibly through a quantum effect in their eyes that turns the field into something they can see. If correct, the finding refigures the navigation problem: not a feat of memory alone but a feat of perception, in which an entire layer of the world becomes available to the animal and not to us.
options: Migratory birds rely on solar and stellar compasses corrected by familiar landmarks, achieving long-distance navigation through a combination of memory and observation.
A quantum effect in birds' eyes lets them detect the Earth's magnetic field, an extraordinary biological mechanism that may eventually be replicated in human navigation devices.
Birds use a combination of celestial cues and the Earth's magnetic field to navigate, with the relative weighting of each cue varying across species and conditions.
If birds can see the magnetic field, migration is less a feat of memory than of perception — a layer of the world available to them that is not available to us.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Pre-literate cultures are sometimes pitied for losing what was not written down. The judgement projects a literate anxiety onto practices that worked on a different principle. Where stories had to be carried in memory, the carrying shaped the stories themselves: epithets, rhyme, recurring scenes — devices that made remembering possible — became part of what was preserved. The result was not a fragile copy of what writing could have done better; it was a different art, with strengths writing tends to lose. Treating it as a deficient first draft of literacy understates the achievement and misreads its purpose.
options: Oral tradition was a different art, not a fragile draft of literacy; its memory devices were part of what it preserved, and reading it as deficient mistakes its achievement.
Oral traditions used epithets, rhyme, and recurring scenes as memory aids, devices that helped reciters carry long stories across generations without written records.
The transition from oral to literate culture involved real losses, since some forms of expression cultivated in oral tradition cannot be recreated in a literate medium.
Modern judgements of pre-literate societies tend to project the values of literate cultures onto them, a habit anthropologists have struggled to overcome in their fieldwork.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Patents are defended as a bargain: a temporary monopoly that gives inventors an incentive to publish what they would otherwise keep secret. The bargain assumes the alternative is concealment. Where industries instead build on continuous incremental improvement — software, much of biotech — the alternative is rapid open iteration, which patents can slow rather than encourage by allowing each tiny step to be fenced off. The system can fit one industry's logic and misfit another's. To debate patents as a single instrument is to assume a single inventive process, where in fact several different ones coexist under the same legal roof.
options: Patents harm innovation in software and biotech because they allow incremental improvements to be fenced off, slowing the rapid open iteration on which those industries actually depend.
Patents reward inventors with a temporary monopoly in exchange for publishing knowledge that would otherwise remain secret, a bargain that has driven innovation for centuries.
Patents fit industries whose alternative is concealment but misfit those built on incremental improvement; treating patents as one instrument assumes a single inventive process that doesn't exist.
The legal framework governing patents should be redesigned to distinguish between industries that depend on secrecy and those that depend on incremental innovation.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Colonial empires made elaborate use of maps not merely to record territories they ruled but to bring those territories into legible existence. The act of surveying — fixing borders, naming districts, plotting roads — converted varied landscapes into administrable units. People who had identified themselves by clan or watershed found themselves listed by a colour on a chart that an official thousands of miles away could read at a glance. Mapping was not the passive reflection of conquest; it was part of the work conquest required, the prior step that made governance from a distance possible.
options: Colonial empires produced detailed maps of the territories they administered, which were essential reference documents for officials in distant capitals.
Mapping by colonial powers reduced varied human identities — clan, watershed, language — to administrative categories that often distorted the realities they were meant to capture.
Maps drawn by colonial administrators were biased and inaccurate, since the surveyors imposed their own categories on landscapes they did not understand.
Colonial mapping was an instrument of conquest, not a passive record of it: by converting landscapes into administrable units it made distant governance possible.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Facial recognition systems are often criticised for being inaccurate, particularly on darker-skinned faces. The criticism is valid, but improving accuracy is not the reassurance it sounds like. A perfectly accurate system in the hands of an authority that surveils selectively does more harm, not less. The deeper problem is not what the technology fails to do but what it lets a state do at scale that was previously expensive enough to constrain. Fixing the bias addresses the symptom most visible to engineers; the political question — when may a state recognise a face — is left untouched.
options: Facial recognition is inaccurate on darker-skinned faces, and engineers must work to fix this bias before the technology is deployed in any public setting.
Improving facial-recognition accuracy does not address the deeper political question of when a state may recognise a face — a question fixing the engineering bias leaves untouched.
Facial recognition systems used by state authorities surveil selectively, which means accuracy improvements would make their selective surveillance even more harmful than at present.
The political question of when a state may recognise a face is more important than any technical question about whether the underlying system identifies faces accurately.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Critics have predicted the death of the novel for over a century — first at the hands of cinema, then television, then the internet. The diagnosis keeps failing because it misunderstands what readers want from novels. Newer media are stronger at spectacle, faster at suspense, and unbeatable at sheer volume. What they cannot do, and have shown no sign of learning, is interiority — the long unhurried inhabitation of another mind. As long as people are curious about what it is like to be someone else, the form that does that best will find readers.
options: Predictions of the novel's death misread what readers want: newer media outdo novels at spectacle and volume but not at interiority, which keeps readers returning.
Critics have predicted the death of the novel for over a century, blaming cinema, television, and the internet in succession for the supposed decline.
Novelists should focus on interiority — the long, slow exploration of another mind — since this is the only thing newer media cannot reproduce.
Cinema, television, and the internet are stronger than the novel at spectacle, suspense, and volume, and these strengths explain why traditional fiction sales have declined.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Confirmation bias — the tendency to credit information that supports one's existing beliefs and discount what contradicts them — is usually described as a defect of reasoning. The label is unhelpful. The same procedure makes sense for an animal that needs a working model of its environment urgently and cannot afford to revise it every time a stray observation arrives. What is irrational in a scientist who should hold beliefs lightly is sensible for a creature whose beliefs are tools for staying alive. Treating the bias as pathology and treating it as adaptation are both right, depending on which task one has in mind.
options: Confirmation bias is irrational in scientific inquiry but is best described as a survival adaptation rather than as a defect of reasoning.
The label 'defect of reasoning' for confirmation bias is unhelpful because the bias is actually useful in everyday life and harmful only in specialised contexts like research.
Whether confirmation bias is pathology or adaptation depends on the task — sensible for an animal needing a stable model, a defect for a scientist who should hold beliefs lightly.
Animals filter new information to preserve a working model of their environment, and humans inherit this tendency from a long evolutionary past in which it served them well.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

A ship has every plank replaced one at a time over the years; the discarded planks are reassembled into a second vessel. Which, if either, is the original? The puzzle invites a verdict and rewards none, because the question assumes an answer to a prior question we never asked: what we mean by a single ship persisting through time. Different criteria — continuity of use, of material, of design, of name — point to different ships, and there is no fact of the matter prior to a choice between criteria. The puzzle is therefore not a discovery about ships but a reminder about identity: the concept is doing work we did not notice it doing.
options: When all the planks of a ship are replaced and the discarded planks reassembled, the reassembled vessel is the original, since material continuity is the most natural criterion of identity.
The puzzle is not about ships but about identity: persistence over time depends on a chosen criterion — use, material, design, or name — with no neutral fact of the matter prior to that choice.
Philosophers have debated the Ship of Theseus for centuries, and the persistence of the disagreement shows that questions about identity admit no rational resolution.
Continuity of use, of material, of design, and of name each offer different answers to which ship is the original, with use being the most defensible criterion in everyday contexts.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Antibiotic resistance is sometimes described as if bacteria were learning to defeat drugs. The framing is misleading. Bacteria mutate constantly, in ways that have nothing to do with the drugs they will later encounter; an antibiotic does not provoke resistance, it merely sieves a population that already contains it. Generous use of the drugs ensures that the resistant remainder is what reproduces. The lesson follows naturally: the rise of resistance is not about how clever bacteria become but about how thoroughly we filter the bacterial world in favour of the lineages we cannot kill.
options: Bacteria learn to defeat antibiotics through repeated exposure, gradually developing resistance mechanisms that frustrate medical treatment.
Resistant bacteria are typically the result of mutations that occur in response to antibiotic exposure, which means responsible drug use can prevent new resistance from arising.
Antibiotic resistance is more common in hospitals than elsewhere because the dense use of drugs there creates strong evolutionary pressure on bacterial populations.
Resistance arises not because bacteria 'learn' but because antibiotics filter a population that already contains mutants — so heavy use of drugs ensures the unkillable lineages dominate.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Rent control is often condemned on textbook grounds: cap prices and supply will fall, helping the tenants now in flats and hurting those who arrive later. The textbook is not wrong about long-run supply, but it tells only part of the story. The mobility of the protected tenants also falls — they cannot easily give up a regulated lease for an unregulated one — and labour markets stiffen as workers stay where their housing is, not where their wages would be highest. The cost of the policy is therefore not only fewer flats but also fewer of the moves that match people to opportunity.
options: Rent control restricts long-run housing supply, as textbooks observe, but also stiffens labour markets by locking protected tenants into flats they cannot easily leave — so opportunity-matching moves also fall.
Rent control is justified despite its long-run supply effects, because the protection it gives current tenants is more valuable than the housing flexibility it suppresses.
Rent control reduces housing supply over the long run, helping existing tenants while hurting those who would otherwise have moved into newly built housing later.
Tenants in rent-controlled flats are unwilling to leave them because the unregulated market is far more expensive, an effect that is well understood but rarely quantified.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

When a language dies, what is lost is sometimes described as a body of vocabulary or a corpus of folk tales. The description, while accurate, falls short. Languages carve up the world differently: distinctions of colour, kinship, motion, evidentiality that one language treats as obligatory another ignores entirely. To lose a language is to lose a way of dividing experience, not merely a way of naming it. Recording the words preserves a list; what is harder to preserve, and impossible to recover once gone, is the practiced habit of cutting the world a particular way.
options: When a language dies, its body of vocabulary and folk tales is lost; preservation efforts therefore focus on recording words and stories before the last speakers pass away.
Languages encode obligatory distinctions of colour, kinship, motion, and evidentiality that differ from one another, making each language a unique record of cultural categories.
Losing a language loses a way of dividing experience, not merely a set of names; the practiced habit of cutting the world a particular way is what recordings cannot preserve.
Linguistic diversity is declining worldwide, and language documentation programmes should be expanded to preserve as many endangered languages as resources permit.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Smart-city projects equip urban infrastructure with sensors that measure traffic, air quality, noise, and crowd density, promising to govern by data. The promise is plausible where what we care about is also what is easy to count. It misleads where the things that matter most — neighbourhood trust, informal commerce, the texture of a public square — produce no data signature. A city that responds only to its sensors learns to optimise the measurable and to neglect what it cannot see. The technology is not neutral; it tilts attention toward the variables it can read.
options: Smart cities improve urban governance by replacing intuition with measurement, allowing administrators to respond rapidly to changes in traffic, air quality, and crowd density.
Neighbourhood trust, informal commerce, and the texture of a public square cannot be measured by sensors, so smart-city programmes will always fail in cities where these features matter.
Cities should equip their public spaces with sensors only after deciding which urban qualities matter most, so that the technology serves a vision of urban life rather than determining it.
Smart-city sensors tilt governance toward what can be measured and away from what cannot be — neighbourhood trust, informal commerce, the texture of a square — so the technology is not neutral.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

A translation that reads as smoothly as a native text gives the comforting impression that nothing has been lost. The smoothness is the loss. Every translator faces a choice: domesticate the source into the target's conventions, or preserve the source's strangeness and risk friction. The first option flatters the reader into thinking the foreign author thinks like them; the second admits that the author does not, and asks the reader to meet the text halfway. Either is defensible. Disguising the choice — pretending translation is transparent — is not.
options: A translator must choose between domesticating a source text and preserving its strangeness; either choice is defensible, but disguising the choice as transparent translation is not.
Translations that read smoothly in the target language create the impression of fidelity but mask the substantial losses that any translation necessarily entails.
Translators should always preserve the strangeness of a source text, asking the reader to meet it halfway rather than disguising the foreignness of the original author.
Domesticating translations are easier for readers but less faithful to the source, while foreignising translations preserve more but demand more attentive engagement from the reader.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Mindfulness, popularised as a remedy for stress, has been the subject of an expanding body of trials. Many report benefits; methodologies, however, vary so widely — different definitions of the practice, varying durations, no agreed comparator — that meta-analyses often disagree with one another. The cumulative picture is therefore not the firm scientific endorsement the popular press supposes, nor the dismissal that sceptics sometimes claim. It is, more accurately, an open scientific question to which clinical interest has run ahead of the evidence base. Caution about specific therapeutic claims is not the same as cynicism about the practice.
options: Mindfulness has been shown to relieve stress in numerous trials, justifying its widespread popular adoption as a non-pharmacological remedy for everyday anxiety.

The evidence on mindfulness remains inconclusive because of widely varying trial methodologies; clinical interest has outpaced the evidence base, leaving an open question — neither endorsement nor dismissal.
Sceptics of mindfulness rightly dismiss the practice's therapeutic claims, since the trials that purport to support it use inconsistent definitions and lack agreed comparators.
Researchers must standardise the definition of mindfulness across trials before any meaningful conclusions can be drawn about its therapeutic effectiveness.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Utilitarianism asks us to choose the action that maximises overall well-being. Its directness is its appeal and its embarrassment. Asked whether one would frame an innocent person to prevent a riot that would kill many, the consistent utilitarian must, after calculation, sometimes say yes. Most of us cannot say yes; we feel that something we are unwilling to do constrains us regardless of the sums. The objection does not refute utilitarianism so much as remind us that our actual moral lives include constraints — duties not to use a person merely as a means — that any complete theory must somehow incorporate.
options: Utilitarianism is wrong because it would, in some circumstances, recommend framing an innocent person to prevent a larger harm, a conclusion most people refuse to accept.
Most people refuse to frame an innocent person even to prevent many deaths, demonstrating that utilitarian reasoning is fundamentally alien to ordinary moral thinking.
The frame-an-innocent objection doesn't refute utilitarianism but reveals constraints — duties not to use a person merely as a means — that any complete moral theory must incorporate.
Utilitarian reasoning serves well as a guide for everyday choices but breaks down in extreme cases where moral intuitions resist the calculations the theory recommends.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Astronomers have long inferred dark matter from how galaxies rotate: the visible stars spin too fast for the visible mass to retain them. Decades of dedicated searches have found no particle that matches the bill. One response is to continue searching with more sensitive instruments. Another, less popular within the field, is to ask whether the inference itself is at fault — whether gravity at galactic scales might not behave exactly as our equations assume. The standoff is interesting not because we know which side will be right, but because it shows how an established absence of evidence can become evidence about the framework that demands it.
options: Decades of failed searches now function as evidence about the framework that demanded the particle — whether gravity at galactic scales obeys our equations — though the field has not settled the matter.
Dark matter does not exist; its non-detection after decades of dedicated searches indicates that the original inference from galactic rotation was based on a mistaken theory of gravity.
Astronomers continue to search for dark-matter particles using ever more sensitive instruments, which represent the most plausible response to decades of negative results.
Inferring dark matter from galactic rotation curves was a reasonable first step, but the field must now move on to alternative theories of gravity that better fit the observations.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Schoolbook accounts of the First World War still place the assassination at Sarajevo at the centre, as if a single bullet had set the continent ablaze. The drama is tidy but it conceals the relevant machinery. By 1914 the European powers had constructed a system of mobilisation timetables so rigid that, once any major army began to move, the others had to begin moving too or lose any chance of victory. The assassination was the spark; the dry timber was decades of treaties and railway schedules that had removed the option of pausing once the match was struck.
options: The assassination at Sarajevo was the principal cause of the First World War, since the murder of Archduke Franz Ferdinand directly precipitated the diplomatic crisis that followed.
Mobilisation timetables in 1914 were so rigid that any pause once mobilisation began would have meant defeat, and this constraint alone determined that war would follow the assassination.
European treaties and alliances built up over decades caused the First World War, by binding the powers into a system from which no peaceful exit was possible after 1914.
Sarajevo was the spark, but the dry timber was decades of treaties and rigid mobilisation timetables that left no option of pause once war began to be triggered.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Behavioural economics is often presented as a corrective to the orthodox model of rational choice, cataloguing the heuristics and biases by which people deviate from textbook predictions. The presentation flatters the field by treating these deviations as anomalies on an otherwise sound map. They are better understood as evidence that the map was drawn at the wrong scale. Real decisions are made under time, attention, and emotional pressures the model does not represent at all; what looks like a bias against rationality is often a sensible adjustment to constraints the rationality criterion ignores.
options: Behavioural economics catalogues the heuristics and biases by which people deviate from rational choice, providing a useful empirical correction to the orthodox model.
The heuristics studied by behavioural economists are sensible adaptations to time and attention constraints, which means orthodox rational choice can be safely retained as the default model.
Behavioural findings are not anomalies on an otherwise sound rational-choice map but evidence the map was drawn at the wrong scale, missing the pressures under which real decisions happen.
Real decisions are made under time, attention, and emotional pressures that the orthodox economic model cannot represent in its current form.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Enthusiasts for cryptocurrency promised a financial system without trusted intermediaries — payments cleared by code rather than by banks. The architecture has worked, in the technical sense, for over a decade. Yet most ordinary users have ended up holding their coins through centralised exchanges that look unsettlingly like the banks they were supposed to replace, complete with executive risk, custodial breaches, and rescues. The technology removed the need for a trusted middleman; the practice has rebuilt the middleman on top of it, because most users prefer convenience to the burdens of self-custody.
options: Cryptocurrency exchanges suffer from custodial breaches and executive risk, problems that traditional banks have largely avoided through regulation.
The crypto protocol removed trusted intermediaries, but in practice users have rebuilt centralised exchanges resembling banks, because most prefer convenience to self-custody.
Self-custody of cryptocurrency is burdensome for ordinary users, who find centralised exchanges more convenient even though those exchanges introduce new risks.
Cryptocurrency has failed in its original mission of removing trusted intermediaries from finance, and should be considered a failed experiment in monetary architecture.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Meritocracy promises that positions of advantage should go to those who earn them, an ideal more attractive than the hereditary alternatives it replaced. Its hidden cost is the moral lesson it teaches the winners. If success is deserved, the successful owe nothing to the unsuccessful, and the unsuccessful are tempted to read their own circumstances as personal failure. A society organised around merit thus produces winners more entitled and losers more demoralised than a society that openly acknowledged the role of luck. The ideal does not fail by missing its target; it fails by hitting it.
options: Meritocracy fails not by missing its target but by hitting it: the better it rewards merit, the more entitled the winners and the more demoralised the losers.
Meritocracy produces winners who feel entitled and losers who feel ashamed, because the system tells both groups their outcomes are deserved rather than influenced by luck.
A society that openly acknowledged the role of luck in life outcomes would be more humane than one organised around the meritocratic ideal of earned reward.
Hereditary systems of social position were less attractive than meritocracy, but at least produced none of the moral pathologies that meritocracy now creates among winners and losers.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Judges granting parole, doctors prescribing antibiotics, and shoppers picking through a long aisle all decide worse late in the day than early. The familiar explanation is depletion: the mind, like a muscle, tires with use. The depletion model has not held up well in replication; what does seem to hold is that the quality of late decisions worsens because attention narrows and the default option grows more attractive than reflection. Calling the effect fatigue captures the slump but misnames the mechanism, and misnamings of mechanism shape the wrong remedies.
options: Judges, doctors, and shoppers make worse decisions late in the day because the mind tires with prolonged use, much as a physical muscle does.
Decision quality declines through the day for judges, doctors, and shoppers, an effect that pervades professional life and demands serious institutional attention.
Late-day decisions worsen not because the mind tires but because attention narrows and defaults overtake reflection — misnaming the mechanism as fatigue invites misdirected remedies.
Replication studies have undermined the depletion model of decision making, although researchers have not yet settled on a replacement that accounts for late-day decision decline.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Critics speak of certain films as realistic when their lighting is muted, their actors unstyled, their dialogue overlapping. The convention is by now so familiar that it operates as a style, instantly readable as the look of the real. What once unsettled audiences with its refusal of polish now soothes them with its predictability. The lesson is older than cinema: a sign of authenticity, repeated long enough, becomes a costume that authenticity can wear. The cure for the worn convention is rarely more conviction, only a new convention not yet recognised as one.
options: Realistic cinema, achieved through muted lighting and overlapping dialogue, is more honest than stylised cinema and should be encouraged by serious filmmakers and critics alike.
The marks of cinematic realism have become a recognisable style; the cure for a worn convention is not more conviction but a new convention not yet read as one.
Cinematic conventions of realism have grown so familiar that audiences now recognise them instantly, leading to a saturation of similar films in contemporary art-house cinema.
Filmmakers seeking authenticity should abandon the worn signs of realism — muted lighting, unstyled acting — in favour of more rigorous documentary methods grounded in real settings.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Existentialism's slogan that existence precedes essence is sometimes mistaken for a celebration of unconstrained freedom: pick the self you want and proceed. The reading misses the burden it was originally meant to convey. If we do not arrive with a nature that decides our purpose, then the responsibility for whatever purpose we adopt is wholly ours, including the responsibility for having taken it on rather than another. The freedom the slogan names is real, but the philosophers who coined it described it as anxiety, not liberation, because the cost of unmade choices cannot be charged to a maker.
options: Existentialist freedom involves no constraints, since we arrive without a fixed nature and may select whatever purpose appeals to us at any moment.
The slogan 'existence precedes essence' has been widely misunderstood by popular readers who lack the philosophical training to grasp its original technical meaning.
Existentialism rightly emphasises that responsibility for one's purpose lies entirely with the individual, since no human nature determines what we should become.
Existentialist freedom is real but burdensome: with no given nature to underwrite our purpose, full responsibility falls on us — its philosophers called it anxiety, not liberation.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

The civil rights campaigns of the mid-twentieth century are remembered for moral arguments and dramatic confrontations. Less visible is the logistical labour: networks of phone trees, mimeographed schedules, training sessions in non-violent response, household-by-household canvassing. The marches that made the news were the visible fraction of an iceberg of organisation that took years to assemble. To describe the movement as a tide of conscience flatters the country it confronted by suggesting that the change required only the right speech. The change in fact required institutions, and institutions take patient and unglamorous building.
options: The civil rights movement succeeded mainly because of the moral power of the speeches and confrontations it produced in the mid-twentieth century.
Logistical labour is essential to any successful social movement, but it is rarely recognised because journalists and historians focus on dramatic public moments rather than backstage organisation.
Crediting the civil rights movement to a tide of conscience obscures the patient, unglamorous institution-building — phone trees, training, canvassing — that the visible marches depended on.
Civil rights campaigners spent years on phone trees, mimeographed schedules, and training in non-violent response, work that took place before the marches that drew public attention.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Mass extinctions in the fossil record were once seen as catastrophic interruptions of an otherwise gradual story of evolution. The newer reading inverts the relationship. The clades that dominate today — flowering plants, mammals, modern birds — became dominant not in spite of the disruptions but because of them: the existing incumbents were knocked aside in a way that competition alone could not manage. Extinctions are therefore not exceptions to evolution but engines of it, opening the space in which the next round of diversification can occur. Without the periodic clear-outs, the world we now inhabit could not have come into being.
options: Mass extinctions are engines of evolution, not interruptions: by clearing incumbents they opened the space in which today's dominant clades — flowering plants, mammals, modern birds — could rise.
Mass extinctions are catastrophic interruptions of the gradual evolutionary process, removing many species but leaving the long-term trajectory of life essentially unchanged.
Flowering plants, mammals, and modern birds became dominant clades after the extinctions of their evolutionary predecessors during the major mass-extinction events.

Competition between species is insufficient to dethrone established incumbents, which is why mass extinctions, rather than ordinary selection, drive most of the major transitions in the history of life.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

The alignment problem in artificial intelligence is often described as the difficulty of teaching a machine to share our values. The phrasing assumes 'our values' name a coherent target. They do not. Humans disagree across cultures, and individuals disagree with themselves on different days. A system trained to follow a stated preference faithfully will still fail us in cases where the preference, examined carefully, is one we would have wished we had not given. The problem is not getting a machine to want what we want; it is that what we want is itself unstable, and we have not yet decided which version of ourselves the machine should obey.
options: AI alignment is the challenge of teaching a machine to share human values reliably, and recent advances suggest the problem will be solved within the next decade.
Alignment is harder than 'make a machine share our values' suggests: what we want is itself unstable, and we have not decided which version of ourselves the machine should obey.
Humans disagree about values across cultures and even within themselves over time, which means that any AI system will inevitably misalign with some users in some circumstances.
Engineers should not attempt to build aligned AI systems until philosophers and ethicists have first reached agreement on a stable, cross-cultural account of human values.

question_text:
The passage given below is followed by four alternate summaries. Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.

Equalising school resources — buildings, books, teacher pay — is treated as the central project of educational equity. The treatment is too narrow. Children arrive at school carrying years of unequal preparation, and what happens for six hours in a classroom must compete with what happens for the other eighteen at home. Even an identically funded school does not deliver an identical opportunity, because the same instruction lands on differently prepared minds. To equalise outcomes through schools alone is to ask the school day to undo a household decade. The ambition is honourable; the lever is too short for the load.
options: Schools cannot deliver equal opportunity through equal resources, so educational policy should focus on transforming early-childhood home environments rather than on equalising school inputs.
Equal school funding does not deliver equal opportunity because students arrive with unequal preparation built up at home, and identical instruction lands on differently prepared minds.
Equalising school resources is an honourable but inadequate lever for educational equity: it cannot undo the years of unequal home preparation that children bring to identical classrooms.
The hours children spend at home shape their educational outcomes more strongly than the hours they spend in school, which means resource-equalisation policies have been misdirected for decades.
